longTailKeywords: when is testicular pain an emergency
_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against AUA scrotal imaging white paper, EAU Urological Infections guideline 2024, CDC STI Treatment Guidelines 2021, DaJusta 2013 torsion review, Levine 2015 chronic scrotal pain review. Drug doses checked against Sanford Guide and UpToDate Lexicomp.

h1Title: Testicular Pain (Orchialgia): Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Testicular pain, medically called orchialgia, ranges from a sudden, severe scrotal emergency to a low-grade ache that drags on for months. Acute onset pain in a teenager or young man is testicular torsion until proven otherwise, a true surgical emergency with a 4 to 6 hour window for testicular salvage. In sexually active adults, acute epididymitis from chlamydia, gonorrhea, or coliform bacteria is the most common cause. Chronic orchialgia, defined as pain lasting three months or longer, affects roughly 100,000 US men annually and frequently follows vasectomy, varicocele, infection, or has no identifiable structural cause. The decisive workup is a focused physical exam, urgent scrotal Doppler ultrasound, and urine plus urethral testing for sexually transmitted infections.

keyStats.prevalence: Acute scrotal pain accounts for approximately 0.5% of pediatric emergency department visits; chronic orchialgia affects 1-5% of urology outpatient visits
keyStats.demographics: Testicular torsion peaks at age 12-18 years (annual incidence 1 in 4,000 males under 25); epididymitis most common ages 14-35 from STI and over 35 from coliforms
keyStats.avgAge: Bimodal — torsion in adolescents, epididymitis and chronic orchialgia spanning ages 20-60
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 3.8 per 100,000 males per year develop testicular torsion globally; epididymitis accounts for 600,000 US clinic visits annually

definition: Testicular pain (ICD-10: N50.81 testicular pain unspecified) is pain perceived in one or both testes, the epididymis, the spermatic cord, or the surrounding scrotum. The term orchialgia is used clinically and is subdivided by duration into acute (less than 6 weeks), subacute (6 weeks to 3 months), and chronic (3 months or longer). Acute scrotal pain is treated as a urological emergency until testicular torsion is excluded because the testis loses viability within hours of complete cord twist. Common acute causes include testicular torsion, acute epididymitis or epididymo-orchitis, mumps orchitis, trauma with hematocele or testicular rupture, incarcerated inguinal hernia, Fournier gangrene of the perineum, and segmental testicular infarction. Subacute or insidious pain raises suspicion of testicular tumor — typically described as a heavy painless mass but capable of presenting with pain when intratumoral hemorrhage occurs. Chronic orchialgia is defined by intermittent or constant scrotal pain of three months or more that disrupts daily activity; recognized contributors include post-vasectomy pain syndrome (1 to 2% of vasectomies), varicocele, spermatocele, chronic epididymitis, prostatitis, and referred pain from ureteric calculi, L1 to L2 nerve roots, or inguinal hernia. In up to a third of chronic cases no structural cause is found and the syndrome is labelled idiopathic chronic orchialgia.

typesClassification.description: Sudden severe scrotal pain. Includes testicular torsion (surgical emergency), epididymitis, epididymo-orchitis, trauma, Fournier gangrene, and incarcerated hernia. Requires urgent evaluation.
Persistent dull or intermittent pain. Differential includes resolving epididymitis, varicocele, spermatocele, missed torsion with remnant, and germ cell tumor presenting with hemorrhage.

Constant or intermittent scrotal pain disrupting daily activity. Includes post-vasectomy pain syndrome, idiopathic chronic orchialgia, referred pain from ureter or lumbar spine, chronic prostatitis-pelvic pain syndrome, and untreated varicocele.

Pain perceived in the testis but originating elsewhere. Common sources include ureteric calculi (kidney stone passing through mid-ureter), L1 to L2 nerve root irritation, inguinal hernia, post-herniorrhaphy nerve entrapment, and hip pathology.

primarySymptoms: Sudden severe one-sided testicular pain that begins over minutes and is associated with nausea or vomiting, particularly in a male under 25, is the classic torsion presentation and demands emergency evaluation within hours.
Gradually worsening pain over 1 to 2 days, often with fever, urethral discharge, or burning on urination, points toward epididymitis or epididymo-orchitis rather than torsion.
A high-riding testis sitting horizontally in the scrotum, with loss of the cremasteric reflex on the affected side, is highly suggestive of testicular torsion on examination.
Relief of pain when the scrotum is gently lifted (a positive Prehn sign) suggests epididymitis, whereas no relief with elevation supports torsion or other surgical pathology.
Scrotal swelling, redness, and warmth developing over hours to days, sometimes with palpable induration along the back of the testis, is typical of epididymitis or orchitis.

A painless or mildly tender firm lump felt within the body of the testis itself, distinct from the epididymis, requires urgent imaging because testicular cancer is the most common solid malignancy in men aged 15 to 35.
Referred pain into the groin, lower abdomen, or flank with associated hematuria or colicky flank pain suggests a passing ureteric stone rather than primary scrotal pathology.

Chronic dull ache or heaviness in one testis lasting three months or longer, worsening with prolonged standing, exercise, or sexual activity, defines chronic orchialgia and often follows vasectomy or accompanies a varicocele.
A bag-of-worms feel of dilated veins above the testis, more obvious on standing or with Valsalva, indicates varicocele and is most common on the left.
Skin necrosis, crepitus, severe perineal pain extending to the scrotum, and systemic toxicity together signal Fournier gangrene, a life-threatening necrotising fasciitis requiring emergency surgery.
earlyWarningSigns: Brief episodes of severe testicular pain lasting minutes that resolve spontaneously — intermittent testicular torsion that can precede a full torsion event
Burning on urination, urethral discharge, or recent unprotected sexual contact with new onset scrotal discomfort
Heaviness, dull ache, or visible vein prominence in the scrotum worsening through the day, particularly in young men with reduced fertility
Persistent dull scrotal ache in the weeks to months after a vasectomy
A firm painless lump in the body of the testis noticed during self-examination or by a partner
emergencySigns: Sudden severe one-sided testicular pain with nausea, vomiting, or a high-riding testis — assume testicular torsion and seek emergency surgical evaluation within hours
Scrotal trauma followed by severe pain, bruising, and a hard or misshapen testis — possible testicular rupture requiring urgent surgical repair
Fever with toxic appearance, scrotal redness extending into the perineum, skin necrosis or crepitus — possible Fournier gangrene, a surgical emergency
Acute scrotal pain with a tender groin lump that cannot be reduced — possible incarcerated or strangulated inguinal hernia
Acute pain with hematuria and severe flank pain — possible obstructing ureteric stone referring to the testis

causes.description: Twisting of the spermatic cord cuts off blood supply to the testis. Most common in adolescents aged 12 to 18 and neonates. Without detorsion within 6 hours, salvage rates fall from over 90% to under 10% by 24 hours. The bell-clapper anatomical variant predisposes by allowing the testis to rotate freely within the tunica vaginalis.

The most common cause of acute scrotal pain in adults. Under age 35, sexually transmitted Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae predominate. Over 35 and in men who practise insertive anal intercourse, Escherichia coli and other coliforms ascend from the urinary tract, often in the context of benign prostatic obstruction.

Blunt injury from sport, assault, or straddle injury can produce simple contusion, hematocele, or rupture of the tunica albuginea. Testicular rupture requires surgical exploration within 72 hours to preserve viability and function.

Mumps virus reaches the testis in 20 to 30% of post-pubertal males infected. Onset is typically 4 to 8 days after parotitis. Coxsackie, EBV, and varicella less commonly cause orchitis. Resurgence of mumps in under-vaccinated populations has made this preventable cause more visible again.

Dilatation of the pampiniform venous plexus, most often left-sided due to perpendicular drainage of the left testicular vein into the renal vein. Present in roughly 15% of adult males and a common contributor to chronic scrotal ache and impaired fertility.

Chronic scrotal pain occurs in 1 to 2% of men after vasectomy, beginning weeks to months after the procedure. Mechanisms include epididymal congestion, sperm granuloma, and nerve injury along the spermatic cord.

causes.cause: Referred pain from ureter, lumbar spine, or hernia
causes.description: The testis shares sensory innervation (T10 to L1) with the kidney, ureter, and lower abdomen. A ureteric stone, L1 to L2 nerve root irritation, inguinal hernia, or post-herniorrhaphy nerve entrapment can produce isolated testicular pain with an entirely normal scrotal exam.

Germ cell tumors are the most common solid malignancy in males aged 15 to 35. Classical presentation is a firm painless intratesticular lump, but 10 to 20% present with pain due to intratumoral hemorrhage. Any persistent intratesticular mass requires ultrasound and tumor markers.

riskFactors.description: Peak window for testicular torsion. Adolescent males presenting with sudden scrotal pain are evaluated as torsion until imaging or surgery rules it out.

A congenital variant in which the tunica vaginalis fully encloses the testis, allowing free rotation. Present bilaterally in most torsion patients and the rationale for bilateral orchiopexy after a torsion event.

riskFactors.factor: Unprotected sexual contact and new partners
riskFactors.description: Markedly raises risk of chlamydial and gonococcal epididymitis. CDC surveillance shows over 1.6 million reported chlamydia cases in the US in 2022, with under-25 men disproportionately affected.

riskFactors.factor: Benign prostatic obstruction and recent urinary instrumentation
riskFactors.description: Predispose to coliform epididymitis in men over 35. Catheter use, cystoscopy, and trans-rectal prostate biopsy are documented triggers.
1 to 2% of men develop chronic post-vasectomy pain syndrome. Risk is independent of surgical technique in most large series.
Shifts the bacterial spectrum of epididymitis toward enteric organisms (E. coli) and influences empiric antibiotic choice per CDC 2021 STI guidelines.
Cycling, martial arts, soccer, and motorcycling account for most blunt scrotal trauma. Hard-shell protective cups significantly reduce serious testicular injury.
Brother or father with testicular germ cell tumor increases personal risk roughly 4 to 8 fold. Cryptorchidism (undescended testis) further compounds risk.
Undescended testis, even if surgically corrected, carries 2 to 8 fold higher risk of testicular germ cell tumor and higher risk of torsion.
MMR vaccine has near eliminated mumps orchitis in fully vaccinated cohorts; outbreaks continue in under-vaccinated populations.

affectedDemographics: Testicular torsion: roughly 3.8 per 100,000 males per year overall, peaking at 1 in 4,000 males under age 25
Acute epididymitis: approximately 600,000 US clinic visits annually; chlamydial cause predominates under age 35, coliform cause over 35
Chronic orchialgia: estimated 100,000 men in the US seek care annually; up to one third of cases are idiopathic
Testicular cancer: 9,760 new US cases in 2024 (American Cancer Society); 95% present age 15 to 44; over 95% 10-year survival when treated early
Post-vasectomy pain syndrome: occurs in 1 to 2% of the 500,000 US vasectomies performed annually
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of testicular pain begins with a focused history and examination aimed at the single most important early decision — whether testicular torsion is in play. Onset, duration, sexual history, urinary symptoms, trauma history, prior similar episodes, and vasectomy status are documented quickly. Examination assesses testicular lie, size, tenderness, the cremasteric reflex, scrotal skin, the epididymis, the spermatic cord, the inguinal region, and the abdomen. Color Doppler scrotal ultrasound is the imaging gold standard recommended by both the American Urological Association and the European Association of Urology. Absent or markedly reduced intratesticular arterial flow is consistent with torsion, while increased flow with an enlarged hyperemic epididymis indicates epididymitis or epididymo-orchitis. Ultrasound also identifies tumors, hematocele, abscess, hydrocele, varicocele, and segmental infarction. When clinical suspicion of torsion is high, surgical exploration must not be delayed for imaging — the testis can be lost within hours. Urinalysis with microscopy and culture is performed in every case; pyuria with positive culture supports bacterial epididymitis. Urethral swab or first-catch urine for nucleic acid amplification testing (NAAT) for Chlamydia and gonorrhea is mandatory in sexually active patients. Complete blood count and inflammatory markers help in suspected orchitis or Fournier gangrene. Tumor markers (alpha-fetoprotein, beta-hCG, lactate dehydrogenase) are ordered when ultrasound shows an intratesticular mass, and a radical inguinal orchiectomy is planned for tissue diagnosis — trans-scrotal biopsy is contraindicated because it seeds tumor along an unnatural lymphatic pathway. For chronic orchialgia lasting three months or longer, the workup expands to include semen analysis if fertility is relevant, urologic ultrasound to exclude varicocele and spermatocele, ureteric and renal imaging if referred pain is suspected, and lumbar spine assessment for L1 to L2 nerve root pathology when the scrotal exam is normal.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard imaging — distinguishes torsion (absent or markedly reduced intratesticular flow) from epididymitis (increased flow, enlarged epididymis), identifies tumors, hematocele, abscess, segmental infarction, hydrocele, and varicocele.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Warm gel and a probe are applied to the scrotum and groin for 15 to 25 minutes. Painless apart from mild tenderness with probe pressure. Reports are usually available the same day in emergency settings.

diagnosticTests.test: Urinalysis with microscopy and urine culture
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects pyuria, bacteriuria, and hematuria. Positive findings support epididymitis or referred pain from urolithiasis; a clean midstream sample tilts away from urinary tract infection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A midstream clean-catch urine sample. Dipstick results are immediate; culture results take 24 to 48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Urethral swab or first-catch urine NAAT for chlamydia and gonorrhea
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the sexually transmitted causes of epididymitis in patients under 35 or with risk factors. Influences antibiotic choice and triggers partner notification.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Either a small swab from the urethra or a first-catch urine sample. NAAT is highly sensitive and specific; results take 1 to 3 days in most labs.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Used when ultrasound shows an intratesticular mass. Elevations support germ cell tumor and inform staging and surveillance.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results usually return within 24 to 48 hours. Markers are also tracked after orchiectomy and during follow-up.

diagnosticTests.test: Complete blood count and inflammatory markers (CRP)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Support diagnosis of infection or Fournier gangrene. Elevated white cell count with high CRP raises concern for abscess or necrotising infection requiring surgical input.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results return within hours in emergency settings.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Diagnostic and therapeutic — definitive when torsion cannot be excluded clinically or imaging is equivocal. Allows detorsion, viability assessment, and bilateral orchiopexy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under general or spinal anesthesia through a midline scrotal incision. Hospital stay is typically same day to 24 hours. Sutures are absorbable; return to non-contact activity in 1 to 2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.test: Ureteric or renal imaging (non-contrast CT KUB or renal ultrasound)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Used when referred pain from a passing ureteric stone is suspected — flank pain, hematuria, colicky pattern with a normal scrotal exam.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-contrast CT of the kidneys, ureters, and bladder takes 5 to 10 minutes. Renal ultrasound is radiation-free and used preferentially in children and pregnancy.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of testicular pain is driven by the underlying cause and tracks the acute versus chronic split. Suspected testicular torsion is a surgical emergency — emergent scrotal exploration with detorsion and bilateral orchiopexy within 6 hours of pain onset achieves over 90% testicular salvage, falling to under 10% by 24 hours per DaJusta 2013 and EAU 2024 data. Manual detorsion (opening-the-book rotation) can be attempted at the bedside while preparing for surgery but does not replace operative fixation. Acute epididymitis is treated empirically before culture results return. Sexually active men under 35 receive ceftriaxone 500 mg intramuscular single dose plus doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for 10 days, covering Chlamydia and gonorrhea per CDC 2021 STI guidelines. Men over 35 or with insertive anal intercourse risk, where coliform bacteria predominate, receive ceftriaxone 500 mg IM plus levofloxacin 500 mg orally once daily for 10 days, or levofloxacin alone in lower STI-risk patients. Bed rest, scrotal elevation with a supporter, ice for the first 48 hours, and an NSAID such as ibuprofen 600 mg three times daily provide symptom relief. Partner notification and concurrent treatment are mandatory in STI-related cases. Testicular trauma with suspected rupture is repaired within 72 hours; conservative management of small hematoceles is reasonable. Suspected testicular tumor is treated by radical inguinal orchiectomy — the testis is removed through a groin incision and the spermatic cord ligated high. Trans-scrotal biopsy is contraindicated because it alters lymphatic drainage and worsens staging. Chronic orchialgia is managed conservatively first. NSAIDs, gabapentin or pregabalin, tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline 10 to 25 mg at night), pelvic floor physiotherapy, and pudendal or spermatic cord nerve blocks form the initial multi-modal approach over 3 to 6 months. Microsurgical denervation of the spermatic cord (MDSC) is offered for refractory chronic orchialgia with a positive response to diagnostic cord block, achieving 70 to 80% durable pain relief in published series (Levine 2015). Varicocelectomy treats varicocele-associated pain in 75 to 90% of carefully selected patients. Vasectomy reversal, epididymectomy, or, as a last resort, orchiectomy are reserved for clearly defined indications and after exhaustive non-surgical trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Emergent scrotal exploration with detorsion and bilateral orchiopexy
medicalTreatments.description: Definitive treatment of testicular torsion. Performed through a midline scrotal incision under general or spinal anesthesia. The affected testis is untwisted, assessed for viability, and fixed to the tunica with non-absorbable sutures; the contralateral side is fixed in the same operation because of bilateral bell-clapper anatomy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Testicular salvage exceeds 90% when surgery occurs within 6 hours of pain onset; falls to roughly 50% at 12 hours and under 10% by 24 hours.

medicalTreatments.name: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose plus doxycycline 100 mg PO twice daily for 10 days
medicalTreatments.description: CDC 2021 STI guideline empiric therapy for acute epididymitis in sexually active men under 35. Covers Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae. Partner notification and concurrent treatment are mandatory.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure in over 90% of uncomplicated cases by 2 weeks; symptoms typically improve within 48 to 72 hours of starting treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM plus levofloxacin 500 mg PO once daily for 10 days
medicalTreatments.description: Empiric therapy for epididymitis in men over 35 or men who practise insertive anal intercourse, where coliform bacteria predominate. Levofloxacin penetrates the prostate well, important in concomitant bacterial prostatitis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure in 85 to 95% of bacterial epididymitis cases; ultrasound resolution lags clinical recovery by weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 600 mg three times daily, naproxen 500 mg twice daily) with scrotal elevation
medicalTreatments.description: Symptom control for epididymitis, orchitis, trauma, and chronic orchialgia. Combined with scrotal support, ice for 48 hours, and rest. Avoid in CKD, peptic ulcer disease, and anticoagulation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meaningful pain reduction in 70 to 85% of patients within 48 hours; modest 20 to 40% improvement in chronic orchialgia.

medicalTreatments.name: Gabapentin 300 to 900 mg three times daily or amitriptyline 10 to 25 mg at night
medicalTreatments.description: Neuropathic pain agents used in chronic orchialgia, particularly post-vasectomy pain or post-herniorrhaphy nerve entrapment. Titrate slowly from low doses. Common side effects include sedation, dry mouth, and weight gain.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinically useful pain reduction in 40 to 60% of chronic orchialgia patients at 3 months.

medicalTreatments.name: Spermatic cord block with local anesthetic
medicalTreatments.description: Both diagnostic and therapeutic. A bupivacaine-lidocaine mixture is injected around the spermatic cord at the external inguinal ring. Pain relief confirms a peripheral pain generator and predicts response to microsurgical denervation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Temporary pain relief in 70 to 80% of chronic orchialgia patients; a positive block predicts roughly 80% durable success with microsurgical denervation.

medicalTreatments.name: Microsurgical denervation of the spermatic cord (MDSC)
medicalTreatments.description: Surgical division of nerve fibres within the spermatic cord while preserving the testicular artery, vas deferens, and lymphatics under microscope magnification. Offered to chronic orchialgia patients with positive diagnostic cord block who have failed medical therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete or substantial durable pain relief in 70 to 80% of well-selected patients (Levine 2015 review).

medicalTreatments.name: Varicocelectomy (microsurgical subinguinal approach)
medicalTreatments.description: Ligation of dilated pampiniform veins for symptomatic varicocele. Microsurgical technique preserves the testicular artery and lymphatics and reduces hydrocele recurrence.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain relief in 75 to 90% of carefully selected patients with confirmed varicocele on examination and ultrasound.

surgicalOptions.name: Radical inguinal orchiectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Standard of care for suspected testicular tumor. Performed through a groin incision with high ligation of the spermatic cord; the testis is delivered without violating the scrotum. Trans-scrotal biopsy is contraindicated because it alters lymphatic drainage and worsens staging.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Curative for stage I germ cell tumor; combined with surveillance, chemotherapy, or radiation depending on histology and stage. Overall 10-year survival exceeds 95% for early-stage disease.

surgicalOptions.name: Testicular rupture repair
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical exploration with debridement of extruded seminiferous tubules and primary closure of the tunica albuginea, ideally within 72 hours of injury. Larger defects may require tunica vaginalis flap coverage.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Testicular preservation rates of 80 to 90% when repaired within 72 hours; falling rapidly after this window.

surgicalOptions.name: Epididymectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of the epididymis for refractory chronic epididymal pain, especially when imaging shows persistent cyst, granuloma, or fibrosis. Spermatic cord block must localise pain to the epididymis preoperatively.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Pain relief in roughly 50 to 75% of selected cases; success depends heavily on accurate preoperative localisation.

surgicalOptions.name: Vasectomy reversal (vasovasostomy)
surgicalOptions.description: Microsurgical reconnection of the vas deferens for post-vasectomy pain syndrome resistant to medical therapy, particularly when epididymal congestion is the suspected mechanism.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Pain relief in 50 to 70% of post-vasectomy pain patients in published case series.

surgicalOptions.name: Orchiectomy for intractable chronic orchialgia
surgicalOptions.description: Last-resort removal of the testis after failure of all non-surgical and conservative surgical options. Inguinal approach is preferred over scrotal because of better outcomes in long-term pain control.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Pain relief in 55 to 75% with inguinal orchiectomy; 40 to 65% with scrotal orchiectomy; counselled only after multidisciplinary review.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pelvic floor physiotherapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Used for chronic orchialgia associated with pelvic floor muscle tension or chronic prostatitis-pelvic pain syndrome. Targeted manual therapy, biofeedback, and stretching reduce referred pain over 8 to 12 weeks.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture and TENS
alternativeTreatments.description: Limited but supportive evidence in chronic orchialgia. Reasonable adjunct to medical therapy when patients prefer non-pharmacological options. Not a substitute for evaluation of treatable structural causes.

whySeeSpecialist: A urologist should be involved when pain persists despite first-line treatment, when imaging shows a testicular mass, varicocele, abscess, or structural abnormality, when chronic orchialgia has lasted three months or longer, when post-vasectomy pain develops, or when fertility evaluation is needed alongside pain workup. Emergency medicine and urology jointly manage acute scrotal emergencies. Any acute scrotal pain with suspicion of torsion warrants emergency department evaluation within hours, not a routine clinic visit.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for board certification in urology and experience with both acute scrotal emergencies and chronic orchialgia management. Microsurgical denervation of the spermatic cord and microsurgical varicocelectomy are sub-specialised procedures — ask how many a surgeon performs per year and what their published outcomes look like. For testicular tumors, choose a center with multidisciplinary genitourinary oncology, including a testicular cancer-experienced medical oncologist and pathologist. Continuity matters in chronic orchialgia management because a multi-modal trial typically takes 3 to 6 months.
preventionStrategies: Wear a hard-shell protective cup for contact sports, cycling, martial arts, and motorcycling to reduce blunt scrotal trauma
Use barrier contraception consistently and limit unprotected partners to reduce risk of sexually transmitted epididymitis
Get the MMR vaccine on schedule (and consider a booster if status is uncertain) to prevent mumps orchitis
Perform monthly testicular self-examination from puberty onward to detect masses early — the most common solid cancer in men 15 to 35
Treat lower urinary tract infections and prostatitis fully to prevent ascending epididymitis, particularly after age 35
Discuss bilateral orchiopexy after any prior torsion event, even if the contralateral testis is currently asymptomatic
lifestyleModifications: Avoid sustained pressure on the perineum from tight clothing, narrow cycle saddles, and prolonged sitting — adopt a split saddle or pressure-relieving cushion if cycling regularly
Use a supportive scrotal supporter during exercise and during acute or chronic painful episodes to reduce traction on the spermatic cord
Stay well-hydrated to reduce ureteric stone risk, a recognised cause of referred testicular pain
Stop smoking — smoking is associated with worse outcomes in chronic pelvic pain syndromes and impairs healing after scrotal surgery
Treat constipation and avoid heavy straining, which aggravates varicocele symptoms and chronic pelvic pain
Plan a calm, gradual return to sexual activity 2 to 4 weeks after vasectomy or scrotal surgery to reduce pain flares

recommended: Adequate fluid intake (2 to 3 liters daily) to reduce ureteric stone formation
Mediterranean or DASH-style eating patterns, which lower urinary stone risk and support general urologic health
Citrus fruits and vegetables for natural urinary citrate, which inhibits calcium stone formation
Foods rich in lycopene and antioxidants (tomatoes, peppers, leafy greens) for general prostate and testicular health
avoid: Excess sodium, which increases urinary calcium and stone risk
Excess animal protein in single sittings, which acidifies urine and raises stone risk
Sugar-sweetened beverages and excessive caffeine, which contribute to dehydration and stone formation
Heavy alcohol intake, which worsens chronic pelvic pain and impairs medication adherence

exerciseGuidelines: Most patients with chronic orchialgia can and should exercise. Low-impact activities (swimming, elliptical, walking) are preferred to running and heavy weightlifting during pain flares. A supportive jockstrap or compression underwear reduces discomfort. After scrotal surgery or torsion repair, avoid heavy lifting and contact sport for 2 to 4 weeks; resume gradually under surgical guidance.
dailyManagement: Use a scrotal supporter during the day when symptoms flare, and elevate the scrotum on a folded towel when resting or sleeping
Apply ice for 15 to 20 minutes several times daily during acute flares of epididymitis or post-procedural pain
Take prescribed antibiotics for the full 10 days even after symptoms resolve, and arrange partner treatment if STI-related
Keep a pain diary noting triggers (sexual activity, exercise, prolonged sitting, stress) — patterns guide chronic orchialgia management
Schedule follow-up ultrasound 4 to 6 weeks after epididymitis to confirm resolution and exclude an underlying mass
prognosis: Prognosis depends sharply on the underlying cause. Testicular torsion treated within 6 hours achieves over 90% salvage and near-normal long-term function; beyond 24 hours, salvage falls below 10% and orchiectomy is usually required. Even salvaged testes can atrophy over months, which is why follow-up ultrasound at 3 to 6 months is standard. Acute epididymitis recovers fully in over 90% of patients within 2 to 4 weeks of appropriate antibiotics, although ultrasound changes lag clinical recovery by weeks. Testicular cancer caught at stage I has a 10-year survival exceeding 95% with radical inguinal orchiectomy and surveillance. Chronic orchialgia is more variable — roughly 30 to 50% of patients achieve substantial relief with combined medical and physical therapy alone, and microsurgical denervation rescues a further 70 to 80% of well-selected refractory cases. The poorer prognostic factors are pain duration over 12 months, prior failed scrotal surgery, untreated depression or anxiety, and a normal physical exam with no identifiable peripheral generator.
recoveryTimeline: Acute epididymitis pain typically falls by half within 48 to 72 hours of starting antibiotics, with full clinical recovery over 2 to 4 weeks. Ultrasound findings normalise over 6 to 12 weeks. Post-scrotal exploration for torsion, most patients return to desk work within 3 to 7 days and to non-contact exercise within 2 to 3 weeks; contact sport at 4 to 6 weeks. Post-orchiectomy recovery is similar. Chronic orchialgia treatment plans run on a 3 to 6 month horizon — a medical trial of 8 to 12 weeks is standard before considering surgical options. Microsurgical denervation patients typically see meaningful pain reduction by 4 to 8 weeks postoperatively.
complications: Loss of the testis (orchiectomy) from delayed torsion presentation — the single most preventable complication when patients seek emergency care within 6 hours
Testicular atrophy after salvaged torsion or severe epididymitis, with impaired endocrine and sperm production on the affected side
Subfertility or infertility from bilateral testicular insult — varicocele, post-infectious obstruction, or bilateral torsion
Chronic orchialgia persisting after the acute episode in 5 to 15% of severe epididymitis patients
Abscess formation, scrotal cellulitis, or Fournier gangrene from untreated or under-treated bacterial infection
Post-vasectomy pain syndrome in 1 to 2% of men undergoing vasectomy
Hydrocele formation after varicocelectomy when lymphatics are not preserved (substantially lower with microsurgical technique)

supportResources.name: American Urological Association — Patient Resources
supportResources.description: Authoritative US patient education on scrotal conditions, torsion, epididymitis, and chronic orchialgia, written and reviewed by board-certified urologists.

supportResources.name: European Association of Urology — Patient Information
supportResources.description: Patient-facing summaries of EAU clinical guidelines on urological infections, scrotal pain, and testicular cancer.

supportResources.name: Testicular Cancer Society
supportResources.description: Patient community, self-examination guides, and survivorship resources for men presenting with testicular masses.

confusedWithConditions.name: Kidney Stones (Nephrolithiasis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A passing ureteric stone refers pain into the testis along shared T10 to L1 innervation. The scrotal exam is entirely normal, and pain comes in colicky waves with flank pain and hematuria. CT KUB shows the stone; scrotal ultrasound is normal.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chlamydia Infection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chlamydia urethritis with testicular pain represents chlamydial epididymitis, the leading cause of acute epididymitis in men under 35. Urethral swab NAAT is positive; treatment is ceftriaxone plus doxycycline. Chlamydia itself can present with urethral discharge and dysuria without scrotal pain.

confusedWithConditions.name: Back Pain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: L1 to L2 nerve root irritation from disc disease or muscle strain can refer pain into the testis with a completely normal scrotal exam. Lumbar pain, positive straight leg raise variants, and improvement with lumbar mobilisation distinguish referred pain from primary scrotal pathology.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pelvic Inflammatory Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PID is the female counterpart of STI-related upper genital infection. In males, the equivalent is epididymitis and prostatitis. Recognising the partner equivalence matters for contact tracing and partner treatment when chlamydia or gonorrhea is the cause.

relatedConditions.name: Chlamydia Infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Leading cause of sexually transmitted epididymitis in men under 35; partner notification and concurrent treatment are mandatory.

relatedConditions.name: Gonorrhea
relatedConditions.relevance: Co-treats with chlamydia in empiric epididymitis regimens; rising fluoroquinolone resistance influences current CDC drug choice.

relatedConditions.name: Kidney Stones
relatedConditions.relevance: Ureteric stones refer pain to the testis along T10 to L1 innervation; a common mimic of primary scrotal pathology.

relatedConditions.name: E. coli Infection
relatedConditions.relevance: The dominant pathogen in epididymitis over age 35 and in men who practise insertive anal intercourse, influencing antibiotic choice.

relatedConditions.name: Back Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: L1 to L2 radiculopathy can refer to the testis with a normal scrotal exam, a common cause of unexplained chronic orchialgia.

relatedConditions.name: Prostate Cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Older men with chronic pelvic and testicular pain warrant prostate-focused evaluation; chronic prostatitis and prostate cancer are both differentials.

faqs.question: When is testicular pain a medical emergency?
faqs.answer: Sudden severe one-sided testicular pain, especially in a male under 25, with nausea, vomiting, or a high-riding testis is a surgical emergency. Testicular torsion has a 4 to 6 hour window for over 90% salvage; salvage rates fall below 10% by 24 hours. Seek emergency care within hours, not the next day.

faqs.question: How is testicular torsion diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines clinical exam findings (high-riding horizontal testis, absent cremasteric reflex, no pain relief with elevation) and urgent color Doppler scrotal ultrasound showing absent or markedly reduced intratesticular blood flow. When clinical suspicion is high, surgical exploration must not be delayed for imaging because the testis can be lost within hours.

faqs.question: What causes one-sided testicular pain in adults?
faqs.answer: Common causes include testicular torsion (especially under 25), acute epididymitis or epididymo-orchitis from sexually transmitted infection or coliform bacteria, scrotal trauma, varicocele, spermatocele, testicular tumor, and referred pain from a ureteric stone or L1 to L2 nerve root irritation. Onset, age, and sexual history guide initial workup.

faqs.question: How is epididymitis treated?
faqs.answer: Sexually active men under 35 receive ceftriaxone 500 mg intramuscularly plus doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 10 days, covering chlamydia and gonorrhea. Men over 35 or with anal intercourse risk receive ceftriaxone plus levofloxacin 500 mg daily for 10 days for coliform coverage. Supportive care includes bed rest, scrotal elevation, ice, and an NSAID.

faqs.question: Can a kidney stone cause testicular pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. A stone passing through the mid-ureter refers pain into the testis along shared T10 to L1 innervation. The scrotal exam is normal, pain comes in colicky waves, and there is often flank pain and visible or microscopic blood in the urine. A non-contrast CT KUB confirms the stone.

faqs.question: What is chronic orchialgia?
faqs.answer: Chronic orchialgia is testicular or scrotal pain lasting three months or longer that disrupts daily activity. Recognised contributors include post-vasectomy pain syndrome, varicocele, chronic epididymitis, prostatitis, and referred pain from spine or ureter. In about a third of cases no structural cause is found and the condition is labelled idiopathic.

faqs.question: Is testicular cancer painful?
faqs.answer: The classical presentation of testicular cancer is a firm, painless lump within the body of the testis, but 10 to 20% of patients present with pain due to intratumoral hemorrhage. Any persistent intratesticular mass, with or without pain, requires urgent scrotal ultrasound and tumor markers. Trans-scrotal biopsy is contraindicated.

faqs.question: How long does pain last after vasectomy?
faqs.answer: Most men have mild scrotal discomfort for 1 to 2 weeks after vasectomy that responds to rest, ice, and ibuprofen. About 1 to 2% develop chronic post-vasectomy pain syndrome, defined as persistent or recurrent scrotal pain beyond three months. Specialist urology evaluation is recommended if pain persists this long.

faqs.question: Can stress or anxiety cause testicular pain?
faqs.answer: Psychological stress does not directly cause testicular pain but commonly worsens chronic pelvic pain syndromes and amplifies pain perception. Anxiety, depression, and pelvic floor muscle tension frequently coexist with chronic orchialgia and respond to combined medical, physical, and psychological treatment.

faqs.question: What is a varicocele?
faqs.answer: A varicocele is dilatation of the pampiniform venous plexus above the testis, most often on the left, present in roughly 15% of adult males. It typically feels like a bag of worms above the testis and is more obvious when standing or straining. It can cause dull scrotal ache, heaviness, and reduced fertility.

faqs.question: Does mumps still cause testicular pain?
faqs.answer: Yes, mumps orchitis still occurs in under-vaccinated populations during mumps outbreaks. Around 20 to 30% of post-pubertal men infected with mumps develop orchitis, usually 4 to 8 days after parotitis. The MMR vaccine on schedule provides near-complete protection and is the principal prevention strategy.

faqs.question: How is testicular trauma evaluated?
faqs.answer: Any significant blunt scrotal injury with persistent pain, swelling, or bruising should be evaluated with scrotal ultrasound to exclude rupture of the tunica albuginea or large hematocele. Testicular rupture is repaired surgically, ideally within 72 hours, to preserve viability. Small contusions and small hematoceles often resolve with rest, ice, and elevation.

faqs.question: What is the Prehn sign?
faqs.answer: The Prehn sign is a bedside test in which the examiner gently lifts the affected scrotum toward the abdomen. Pain relief with elevation suggests epididymitis, while no relief or worsening pain supports torsion or another surgical cause. The test is supportive only and never substitutes for ultrasound or surgical evaluation.

faqs.question: Can chronic testicular pain be cured with surgery?
faqs.answer: Microsurgical denervation of the spermatic cord achieves durable pain relief in 70 to 80% of well-selected chronic orchialgia patients who have a positive response to diagnostic cord block and have failed medical therapy. Varicocelectomy helps 75 to 90% with varicocele-related pain. Surgery follows, not replaces, a structured medical trial.

faqs.question: Should I get my testicles checked regularly?
faqs.answer: Monthly testicular self-examination from puberty onward is recommended by the American Cancer Society to detect lumps early. Stand in a warm shower, gently roll each testis between thumb and fingers, and note any firm lump or change in size or shape. Any persistent finding warrants prompt evaluation.

faqs.question: What does a positive cremasteric reflex mean?
faqs.answer: Stroking the inner thigh normally produces a brisk upward contraction of the same-side testis — a positive cremasteric reflex. Absence of this reflex on the painful side has roughly 60 to 70% sensitivity for testicular torsion in adolescents. A normal reflex does not exclude torsion entirely.

faqs.question: Can I have sex with epididymitis?
faqs.answer: Sexual activity should be avoided until antibiotics have been completed, pain has resolved, and partner treatment has been arranged in STI-related cases. Resuming earlier risks transmission and aggravating epididymal inflammation. Most patients are comfortable resuming activity 2 to 3 weeks after starting effective antibiotics.

faqs.question: What is Fournier gangrene?
faqs.answer: Fournier gangrene is a rapidly progressive necrotising soft tissue infection of the perineum and scrotum, more common in men with diabetes, alcohol use, or immunocompromise. It presents with severe perineal pain, skin discolouration, crepitus, and systemic toxicity. It is a surgical emergency requiring rapid debridement, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and intensive care.

faqs.question: Does testicular pain affect fertility?
faqs.answer: Most acute self-limited causes of testicular pain do not affect fertility. Risks rise with delayed torsion (atrophy of the affected testis), severe bilateral epididymo-orchitis (post-infectious obstruction), bilateral mumps orchitis, and untreated varicocele. Semen analysis is reasonable in young men with chronic pain or after a major acute insult.

faqs.question: When should I see a urologist for testicular pain?
faqs.answer: See a urologist if scrotal pain has lasted more than 2 weeks, recurs after antibiotic treatment, follows vasectomy, is associated with a lump or swelling, accompanies abnormal urine results, or has persisted three months or longer despite primary care therapy. Acute severe pain warrants emergency department evaluation rather than a routine clinic visit.

metaDescription: Testicular pain: sudden severe scrotal pain may be torsion, a surgical emergency. Causes, diagnosis, and treatment reviewed by our medical board for men.
